steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank fills the phrase "any name chosen needs to be ______ that body." The very next clause explains the constraint: "no two minor planets are allowed to have the same name." The word must mean that a name belongs to one body and no other.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — exclusive to: "Exclusive to" means belonging only to that one body — a name exclusive to a planet cannot be shared, which is exactly what the no-duplication rule requires. ✓
  • Choice B — evocative of: "Evocative of" means suggesting or calling to mind. A name could be evocative of a body while still being shared by another — this does not enforce uniqueness. ✗
  • Choice C — typical for: "Typical for" means characteristic or representative. This describes what kind of name fits, not whether the name is unique, so it doesn't satisfy the stated rule. ✗
  • Choice D — superseded by: "Superseded by" means replaced or made obsolete by something. A name being superseded by a body makes no logical sense in this context. ✗
That matches Choice A — exclusive to.
